quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 9
quiz.subtitle: Probability (Advanced Applications)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Complex probability scenarios and conditional reasoning

questions.question: A researcher is studying the efficacy of a new fertilizer. In a sample of 150 plants, 90 were given the fertilizer and 60 were not. Of those given the fertilizer, 72 showed significant growth. Of those not given the fertilizer, 12 showed significant growth. If a plant is chosen at random and it showed significant growth, what is the probability it was not given the fertilizer?
questions.explanation: Total plants with significant growth = $72$ (fertilized) + $12$ (not fertilized) = $84$. The number of those that were not fertilized is $12$. The conditional probability is $12/84$, which simplifies to $1/7$.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 4 times. What is the probability that the coin lands on heads at least once?
questions.explanation: The probability of heads at least once is $1 - P(\text{no heads})$. The probability of tails on all 4 flips is $(1/2)^4 = 1/16$. Therefore, $1 - 1/16 = 15/16$.

questions.question: In a bag of 20 jellybeans, 8 are cherry, 7 are lime, and 5 are lemon. If two jellybeans are selected at random without replacement, what is the probability that both jellybeans are cherry?
questions.explanation: The probability the first is cherry is $8/20$. The probability the second is cherry is $7/19$. Multiply the probabilities: $(8/20) \times (7/19) = 56/380$. Simplifying by dividing both numerator and denominator by 4 gives $14/95$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of membership in two school clubs.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Category} & \text{Chess Club} & \text{Debate Club} & \text{Both Clubs} & \text{Neither Club} \\ \hline \text{Number of Students} & 15 & 20 & 10 & 55 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a student is selected at random from the 100 students surveyed, what is the probability that the student is a member of exactly one club?
questions.explanation: The table lists total club memberships. Chess only = $15 - 10 = 5$. Debate only = $20 - 10 = 10$. Students in exactly one club = $5 + 10 = 15$. The probability is $15/100$.

questions.question: A computer generates a random integer from 1 to 50, inclusive. What is the probability that the generated integer is a multiple of 5 or a multiple of 7?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 5: {5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50} (10 values). Multiples of 7: {7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49} (7 values). The value 35 is counted in both. Using inclusion-exclusion: $10 + 7 - 1 = 16$. The probability is $16/50$.

questions.question: A medical clinic tested 200 patients for a specific gene. The results showed that $15\%$ of the patients carried the gene. If 2 patients are selected at random from the 200 tested, without replacement, what is the probability that both patients carry the gene?
questions.explanation: Number of patients with the gene: $0.15 \times 200 = 30$. Probability the first has it: $30/200$. Without replacement, probability the second has it: $29/199$. Multiply: $(30/200) \times (29/199) = 870/39{,}800$, which simplifies to $87/3{,}980$.

questions.question: A fair eight-sided die, with faces numbered 1 through 8, is rolled twice. What is the probability that the sum of the two rolls is greater than 14?
questions.explanation: There are $8 \times 8 = 64$ total outcomes. Pairs that sum to 15: (7,8) and (8,7). Pairs that sum to 16: (8,8). There are 3 favorable outcomes. The probability is $3/64$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of trees in a local park by species and age group.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Young} & \text{Mature} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Oak} & 14 & 26 & 40 \\ \hline \text{Maple} & 18 & 22 & 40 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 32 & 48 & 80 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a mature tree is selected at random, what is the probability that it is a Maple tree?
questions.explanation: Focus on the 'Mature' column. The total number of mature trees is 48. Out of these, 22 are Maples. The conditional probability is $22/48$, which simplifies to $11/24$.

questions.question: An airline reports that $90\%$ of its flights arrive on time. If 3 flights are chosen at random, what is the probability that at least one of the flights arrives late?
questions.explanation: The probability of at least one late flight is $1 - P(\text{all on time})$. $P(\text{all on time}) = 0.90^3 = 0.729$. Thus, $1 - 0.729 = 0.271$.

questions.question: In a survey of 150 coffee shop customers, 60 ordered a latte, 45 ordered a muffin, and 15 ordered both. If a customer is selected at random, what is the probability that the customer ordered a latte given that they did NOT order a muffin?
questions.explanation: Total customers = 150. Customers who did NOT order a muffin = $150 - 45 = 105$. Customers who ordered a latte and no muffin = $60 - 15 = 45$. The conditional probability is $45/105$, which simplifies to $3/7$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently navigate complex conditional probabilities, apply inclusion-exclusion logic, and correctly identify restricted sample spaces.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on carefully reading the 'given' condition in probability problems, and remember to subtract overlaps when calculating 'A or B'.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: 'at least one' is best solved using the complement rule ($1 - P(\text{none})$), and conditional probability always uses the condition as the new denominator.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by drawing Venn diagrams or two-way tables for every problem. Clearly label your total, your target group, and your overlap, then translate 'and'/'or'/'given' into the correct mathematical operations.
